currentSnapshot.definition: Very bad or unpleasant; used informally as an intensifier.
currentSnapshot.usageNote: Used casually to express strong disapproval or as an amplifier: 'awfully kind', 'an awful lot'.
currentSnapshot.exampleUsage: The weather was awful yesterday, but the cake was awfully good.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Inspiring profound fear or dread; terror-inducing, especially of divine or supernatural power.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: Used almost exclusively in religious and supernatural contexts to describe the wrath of God or the presence of the holy.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: The awful judgment of God descended upon the sinful.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Inspiring reverential awe or deep wonder; worthy of profound respect.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: Shifted from pure terror to reverential awe—used for sublime natural phenomena, holy relics, and great rulers.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: The awful silence of the great cathedral filled every soul with wonder.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Strikingly impressive; commanding deep respect or reverence through sublimity or grandeur.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: Used by poets and writers for natural sublime: mountains, storms, vast silences. Carried weight and gravity.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: The awful majesty of the storm-tossed sea.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Inspiring awe; impressively large, notable, or solemn. Beginning to weaken colloquially.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: Still used seriously in literature, but colloquial speech began using 'awful' as a mere intensifier: 'an awful bore', 'awfully sorry'.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: The awful splendour of the Alps; she was awfully kind about the whole business.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Extremely bad, unpleasant, or of poor quality. The reverential sense is now archaic in everyday speech.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: Pejoration complete in informal registers. 'Awfully' survives as ironic intensifier for positives ('awfully nice').
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: What an awful film — we left at the interval.

summaryOfChange.shortSummary: From awe-inspiring reverence to casual intensifier for 'very bad'.
summaryOfChange.longSummary: Awful began as a deeply reverential Old English adjective meaning 'inspiring fear of God or the divine'. Through Middle and Early Modern English it softened into a term for the sublime — great mountains, solemn silences, powerful rulers. By the Victorian era colloquial usage began to weaken it into a mere intensifier. By the 20th century pejoration was complete: 'awful' now primarily means 'very bad', though the intensifier ghost persists in 'awfully kind'.

keyDates.label: Johnson's Dictionary
keyDates.significance: Samuel Johnson defined 'awful' as 'that which strikes with awe; that which fills with reverence', cementing the reverential sense in the literary canon.

keyDates.label: OED First Edition
keyDates.significance: The first edition of the Oxford English Dictionary documented the colloquial weakening, noting the shift from reverential to merely emphatic use.

keyDates.label: Mid-Century Colloquialisation
keyDates.significance: By 1950, dictionaries had demoted the reverential sense to 'archaic', marking the completion of pejoration in standard usage.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary – awful, adj.
sources.attribution: Paraphrase and excerpt. OED content © Oxford University Press. Used for educational commentary.
sources.excerpt: Originally (of things): Inspiring awe; causing dread, profoundly impressive. Of persons: Commanding profound respect. Later weakened to a general term of disparagement.
sources.relevanceNote: The OED provides dated quotations spanning over a thousand years of English usage, making it the primary authority on this transition.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary – awful, adj. (modern senses)
sources.attribution: Paraphrase. OED content © Oxford University Press.
sources.excerpt: colloquial. Notably bad, unpleasant, or objectionable; 'terrible'. Also as intensifier: 'awfully' = extremely.
sources.relevanceNote: Documents the completed pejoration and surviving intensifier use.

sources.title: A Dictionary of the English Language
sources.author: Samuel Johnson
sources.excerpt: AWFUL. adj. [awe and full.] That which strikes with awe, or fills with reverence.
sources.relevanceNote: Johnson's definition exemplifies the word at the peak of its reverential meaning, before Victorian weakening.

sources.title: An Anglo-Saxon Dictionary
sources.author: Joseph Bosworth, T. Northcote Toller
sources.excerpt: egeful: awful, dreadful, terrible. Used of divine wrath and supernatural dread.
sources.relevanceNote: Documents the Old English root sense of dread and terror associated with the divine.

sources.title: Middle English Dictionary
sources.attribution: © University of Michigan. Used for educational commentary.
sources.excerpt: awful: inspiring awe, reverence, or wonder; (of God, the Church, rulers) commanding deep respect.
sources.relevanceNote: Shows the transition from pure dread to reverence during the Middle English period.

relatedWorks.title: The Prelude
relatedWorks.creator: William Wordsworth
relatedWorks.whyRelevant: Contains numerous canonical uses of 'awful' in the sublime/reverential sense, showing how Romantic poets used the word at its height before pejoration.

relatedWorks.title: A Philosophical Enquiry into the Origin of Our Ideas of the Sublime and Beautiful
relatedWorks.creator: Edmund Burke
relatedWorks.whyRelevant: Burke's theory of the sublime provides the philosophical context in which 'awful' carried its most powerful meaning—the terror-tinged reverence of the Romantic era.

relatedPages.title: Pejoration
relatedPages.summary: How and why word meanings become more negative over time.

relatedPages.title: Semantic Drift
relatedPages.summary: The broad phenomenon of word meanings shifting across time.

relatedConcepts.label: terrible
relatedConcepts.note: Underwent nearly identical pejoration: from 'terror-inspiring' to 'very bad'.

relatedConcepts.label: dreadful
relatedConcepts.note: From 'worthy of dread/awe' to a general negative intensifier.

relatedConcepts.label: sublime
relatedConcepts.note: The Romantic concept of the sublime is where 'awful' had its most powerful reverential meaning.

timelineEvents.title: Divine Dread: the original awful
timelineEvents.summary: In Old English, 'egeful' (awful) described the terror of God's wrath and supernatural power. The word carried the full weight of fear.

timelineEvents.title: Reverential Awe
timelineEvents.summary: The sense softened from pure terror to reverential wonder, applicable to cathedrals, holy relics, and sublime natural scenes.

timelineEvents.title: Johnson's Dictionary codifies the reverential peak
timelineEvents.summary: Samuel Johnson's landmark dictionary defined 'awful' as 'that which strikes with awe', marking the apex of the word's reverential meaning.

timelineEvents.title: The Sublime and the Awful
timelineEvents.summary: Romantic poets—Wordsworth, Shelley, Keats—deployed 'awful' for sublime landscapes and cosmic grandeur. Meanwhile, colloquial speech began using it as a mere emphatic.

timelineEvents.title: OED documents the weakening
timelineEvents.summary: The first edition of the Oxford English Dictionary noted the colloquial weakening of 'awful' to a mere intensifier—marking the beginning of the documented pejoration.

timelineEvents.title: Pejoration complete
timelineEvents.summary: By the mid-20th century, the reverential sense had been demoted to 'archaic' in most dictionaries. 'Awful' now primarily meant 'very bad'.
